header.title: 2026-03-12 – Trade Value and Volume Network Analysis
header.summary: Total trade value reached $4.2B for the week ending 2026-03-12, representing a 5.4% decline compared to the prior period's $4.4B baseline. Volume units remained stable, indicating pricing pressures rather than physical supply chain disruptions.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.content: For the week ending 2026-03-12, the network experienced a contraction in overall trade value USD, primarily driven by export reductions in the West region. While physical volume units remained within 1.2% of the 4-week rolling average, the divergence between value and volume suggests localized commodity price shifts. External context, including recent tariff adjustments on machinery, heavily influenced these financial metrics.

sections.title: Key Findings
sections.content: Recent weekly trends reveal significant localized impacts across specific regions and commodity categories, despite stable overall volume units.

insights.title: Machinery Exports Decline Sharply
insights.details: Trade value USD for HS2-84 (Machinery) exports fell to $850M, a 12.5% drop compared to the prior week's $971M baseline. This $121M contraction correlates strongly (r=0.88) with recent tariff implementations at West Coast ports. Despite the financial drop, volume units only decreased by 2.1%, indicating exporters are absorbing price impacts to maintain market share.

insights.title: Energy Imports Surge in South
insights.details: The South region recorded a $320M increase in HS2-27 (Energy) imports, reaching $1.45B compared to the prior period's $1.13B baseline. This 28.3% week-over-week value expansion was accompanied by a 450,000 unit increase in physical volume. The surge offsets losses in other sectors and highlights shifting regional dependency on imported energy commodities.

insights.title: Northeast Port Volumes Contract
insights.details: Northeast regional ports experienced a 15.4% decline in total trade value USD, dropping from $650M in the prior week to $550M. Physical volume units simultaneously fell by 125,000 units, generating a z-score of -2.4 against the 8-week historical average. This synchronized drop in both value and volume aligns with recent severe weather disruptions delaying maritime arrivals.

sections.title: Forward Outlook
sections.content: Based on current trajectory and historical tariff shock patterns, total trade value USD is expected to stabilize between $4.1B and $4.3B over the next two weeks. Best case scenario: Machinery export values recover to the $950M baseline if alternative markets absorb the redirected volume units. Worst case scenario: The Northeast weather delays cascade, causing an additional $200M contraction in import values next week. Leading indicators to monitor include daily vessel arrival logs at West Coast ports (typically a 4-day leading indicator for trade value realization) and spot energy prices.
